Twitter @tomaarsen
summary:
Model details:
- LateOn-Code: 149M late interaction model, matches Qwen3-embedding-0.6B performance
- Sequence length for documents: 2048
- Output dimensionality: 128
- Apache 2.0 license
- Trained on English & code

🧵

title: Twitter @tomaarsen
summary:
These late-interaction models output multiple vectors for each document: one small embedding per token, and then compared with the MaxSim function. 

This model archetype can reach much higher performance than standard single-vector embedding models.

🧵 https://t.co/0BaPS03ByD

title: Twitter @lateinteraction
summary:
Your periodic reminder that late interaction isn’t “awesome but takes a lot of space” as I see here often.

ColBERT vectors are often 10 bytes each. Ten bytes. That’s like 3-4 floats.

It’s about *interactions* (aka ~attention) not “many vectors”.

It’s not “many vectors work

title: Twitter @antoine_chaffin
summary:
A nice write-up on why late interaction models are much more than simply dense models with one vector per token
It is actually very hard to get good intuitions about an embedding space and associated similarity search, and even more to build one without getting lost in pitfalls

title: Twitter @lateinteraction
summary: A lesson reconfirmed dozens of times in IR: granular scoring generalizes far better than dense dot products when tasks are hard. Late interaction ftw!

title: Twitter @lateinteraction
summary:
Late interaction (ColBERT) is simple.

Let's *not* cram docs into a vector. Instead, let's make the attention (interaction) scalable.

How? Build a *late* interaction fn.

(1) Applied after, not during, encoding.
(2) Pruning-capable, i.e. scales better than linear. This is key! https://t.co/m7fS15SdIE

title: Twitter @lateinteraction
summary: @raphaelsrty @vespaengine @jobergum
